1:
The witnesses read the opening verse as the collapse of a house already tottering. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry surveys the weakness of Saul's whole line: Ishbosheth, who held the throne, drew all his strength from Abner's support, and now that the general lies dead his hands are feeble and no spirit is left in him. The rival house is reduced further still, for its truer heir, Jonathan's son Mephibosheth, is a lame child (v.4) unfit for court or camp. Henry draws the lesson he presses on every irresolute soul: when the difficulties that should sharpen our endeavours dispirit us instead, we betray both our crowns and our lives, and the idle soul becomes an easy prey to the destroyer.

The reformed Gill supplies the identification the verse leaves implicit — 'Saul's son' is Ishbosheth, and the tidings that reached him were that his commander Abner had been murdered by Joab in Hebron (2 Sam. 3:27), the very city where David reigned. That Abner should fall in David's own town could only deepen the alarm, for the one man who had held the northern cause together was gone, and gone by violence.

The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the two effects the verse records, and separate them carefully. Ellicott distinguishes the king from his people: for Ishbosheth 'his hands were feeble,' the whole support and strength of his throne being gone; for the people, they were 'troubled' because they had been carrying on negotiations with David through Abner, and these were now thrown into confusion, so that it became uncertain how they might result. Clarke reads the notice the same way — Abner was their great support, on whom they wholly depended — and takes the verse as plain evidence that Ishbosheth was a feeble prince, possessing few of the qualities requisite in a sovereign.

The voices do not contend; together they make this verse the quiet hinge of the story. The strength of Saul's cause had never lain in Saul's son but in Saul's captain, and with Abner gone the kingdom of the north is a body without sinew. What Henry presses pastorally the historians confirm from the politics of the hour: a throne propped on a single man's arm cannot survive that arm's removal, and the fear that now spreads through Israel prepares the way for the treachery that follows in v.2.
2:
The verse introduces the two assassins, and the witnesses read the seemingly dry genealogy as an indictment loaded in advance. The grammatical-historical commentators make the parenthesis about Beeroth do the moral work. Keil and Delitzsch note that the remark 'Beeroth also was reckoned to Benjamin' is added precisely to confirm that Baanah and Rechab were Benjaminites, and so 'shows the horrible character of the crime attributed to them': two men of the tribe of Benjamin murdered the son of Saul, the king belonging to their own tribe. Ellicott presses the same point — Beeroth was one of the four cities of the Gibeonites (Josh. 9:17), allotted with the rest to Benjamin (Josh. 18:25), and identified with the modern El-Bireh some nine miles north of Jerusalem — and he too observes that 'the fact that the murderers of Ish-bosheth were of his own tribe is made prominent.' The past tense, 'was reckoned,' Ellicott adds, betrays that the town was no longer inhabited when the historian wrote.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke fastens instead on the phrase 'captains of bands,' recalling that the Vulgate and Syriac render it captains of banditti or robbers. He wonders whether Ishbosheth kept troops of marauders whose trade was to make sudden incursions and carry off grain and cattle; and he draws the grim inference that men bred to such work 'would be well qualified for the bloody work in which these two men were afterwards employed.' The office already hints at the outrage.

The reformed Gill is more restrained, taking the two simply as captains of troops in the army, or of the guards about the person of Ishbosheth himself — which, if so, makes their treachery the fouler, since the men set to protect the king become the men who kill him.

The voices do not divide; each angle deepens the same horror. Whether one weighs their tribe with Keil and Ellicott, their trade with Clarke, or their trust with Gill, the verse is quietly stacking the charges: Benjaminites against their own king, soldiers against the master they served, hardened captains against a sleeping man. The genealogy is not filler but the first line of the accusation that David will bring in v.11.
3:
This verse pauses over a displaced people, and the witnesses treat the note as both a historical footnote and a mark of the book's antiquity. The grammatical-historical commentators do the geography. Keil and Delitzsch observe that Gittaim reappears among the towns of returning Benjaminites in Neh. 11:33, though that need not prove it lay within Benjamin's border; the reason the Beerothites fled and 'were strangers there unto this day' is unknown, but they suppose the Philistines may have overrun the region. Ellicott concurs that neither the cause of the flight nor the place can be certainly fixed, judging it probable that the Beerothites fled from Philistine incursions, and he weighs 'until this day' as a sign that 'the time of the writer was not very far removed from the events which he relates.' Clarke, more briefly, guesses that Gittaim is only another form of Gath, as Ramathaim is of Ramah.

The reformed Gill fills in the occasion the others leave open. He reads the flight against the disaster that closed Saul's reign: when Saul fell, many Israelites deserted their cities and abandoned them to the Philistines, and the men of Beeroth, whose town stood near the Philistine country, forsook it and withdrew to Gittaim, a city in the same tribe but set a little further from the enemy. The migration was an act of self-preservation in a losing war.

The witnesses do not dispute one another; the small notice serves them chiefly as a window on the writer. The phrase 'unto this day' tells us the displacement outlasted a generation, and that the historian stands near enough to the events to know that the exiles never went home. Whatever drove them out — Philistine pressure by Gill's and Ellicott's reading, or Keil's conjectured conquest — the effect is the same: Beeroth stands empty, its people scattered, and out of that scattered remnant come the two captains whose crime the chapter is about to record. Even the map is heavy with the ruin that the death of Saul brought upon Israel, the same ruin that has left Ishbosheth exposed in v.5.
4:
The narrative breaks off to remember a lame child, and the witnesses agree that the digression is placed here for a reason: to show that with Ishbosheth the house of Saul is effectively at an end. The grammatical-historical commentators state the design plainly. Keil and Delitzsch say the historian inserts the remark 'to show at the outset, that with the death of Ishbosheth the government of this family necessarily became extinct, as the only remaining descendant was a perfectly helpless cripple.' They record the pathos of the accident — five years old when the news of Saul and Jonathan came from Jezreel, snatched up by his nurse, and lamed in her hurried flight — and note that the boy called Mephibosheth here is the Meribbaal of 1 Chr. 8:34. Ellicott makes the same case: Mephibosheth is introduced to prove the house of Saul 'practically extinct,' for the other descendants were either illegitimate or of the female line (2 Sam. 21:8), leaving none to claim the throne.

The wesleyan-arminian voices read the same fact as the assassins' motive. Wesley says the history is inserted 'as that which encouraged these men to this wicked murder': Saul's family was now at so low an ebb that, once Ishbosheth was dispatched, none would remain but a lame child unfit to govern, so that the crown must fall to David by their act — for which they promised themselves no small reward. Clarke's eye is on the boy himself: the fall dislocated ankle, knee, or thigh, never afterward set, and he breaks into lament — 'Lovely Jonathan! unfortunate in thy life, and in thy progeny.'

The reformed Gill adds only that Jonathan was Saul's eldest son, dead with his father the same day, so that this crippled grandson is what the covenant-friend of David has left behind. The witnesses do not divide; together they turn a broken ankle into a hinge of history. The one prince who might have rallied Saul's cause cannot stand, and the reader is quietly told that the murder of v.7 will settle a succession that was already, in God's providence, decided.
5:
The scene of the crime is set at noon, and the witnesses give most of their attention to the single incriminating circumstance: the king was asleep. The grammatical-historical commentators reconstruct the setting. Keil and Delitzsch place the sons of Rimmon travelling to Mahanaim, where Ishbosheth resided (2 Sam. 2:8), and arriving 'in the heat of the day (at noon) into Ishbosheth's house, when he was taking his mid-day rest.' Ellicott and Clarke explain the custom that makes the detail natural: in hot countries men take a siesta at midday, and, as Ellicott adds, the king's bed would have been in 'the coolest and most retired part of the house' — which is exactly why he lay so exposed and so alone.

The reformed Gill simply carries the two brothers, Rechab and Baanah, to the door, but the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry turns the noonday sleep into a sermon. He notes that the land was not so hot as to compel rest at noon, and so reads the king's bed as the mark of a sluggish man who 'loved his ease and hated business.' At the very crisis of his fortunes, when he should have been at the head of his forces in the field or at the head of his counsels in treaty with David, Ishbosheth was lying asleep, for his hands were feeble (v.1) and so were his head and heart. Henry gathers the warning: 'Love not sleep, lest thou come to poverty and ruin. The idle soul is an easy prey to the destroyer.'

The witnesses do not contend; the historians supply the climate and Henry supplies the conscience. What was, on the surface, an innocent custom of the country becomes, in this man at this hour, a fatal negligence. A king who slept while his kingdom crumbled had already half-surrendered, and the assassins of v.6 have only to walk in. The very arrangement that gave Ishbosheth his rest — a bed set in the quietest, most withdrawn corner of the house — gave the killers their cover, so that the comfort of the noon becomes the occasion of the crime. The verse thus prepares the reader both for the ease of the murder and for the mingled pity and contempt with which the story weighs Saul's last son, a man overtaken not on the battlefield but in his own drowsy chamber.
6:
The murderers gain entry under a household pretext, and the witnesses concentrate on how the assassins reached a king. The grammatical-historical commentators explain the ruse. Keil and Delitzsch read the clause as circumstantial — the men came 'into the midst of the house, fetching wheat,' that is, under the pretext of fetching wheat, probably for the soldiers in their companies — and then 'smote him in the abdomen.' They note that the Septuagint, uneasy at how the pair got in, added a portress who was cleaning wheat and fell asleep, letting the brothers slip past; Ellicott records the same curious Greek addition and glosses the blow 'under the fifth rib' as a stroke to the abdomen, the very wound Abner had dealt Asahel (2 Sam. 2:23). Clarke makes the pretext wholly plausible: the king's stores stood near his dwelling, the captains were accustomed to draw provisions from them, and there was an easy passage to the king's chamber — likely a summer-house — so that no one would suspect their errand.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley fills in the economy behind the excuse: the wheat was laid up in the public granaries in the king's house and drawn thence by the captains for the pay of their soldiers, who in those days were paid not in money but in corn; on this pretence the men were admitted and passed from room to room to the place where the king lay.

The reformed Gill supposes the guards asleep or off duty, or else deceived by the familiar sight of officers come for grain, so that nothing obstructed the pair as they went to the inmost part of the house. The witnesses do not divide; they combine into a single, chilling reconstruction. The horror of the deed lies partly in its ordinariness — a routine visit for wheat, a drowsy noon, an open door — and the very familiarity that should have made the captains trustworthy is the thing that lets them kill. Clarke presses this: because the men were in the daily habit of going to the stores, and the passage from granary to bedchamber was short and unguarded, no one would suspect their present errand, and the crime needed no forced entry, only a false face. The repeated detail of the wound and the escape prepares for the fuller account in v.7, where the head is taken and the flight begins.
7:
The narrative circles back to complete the crime, and the witnesses first account for the apparent repetition and then follow the grisly journey it introduces. The grammatical-historical commentators treat the double telling as good Hebrew narrative, not confusion. Ellicott insists there is 'no difficulty with the repetition,' since Scripture commonly repeats a statement when some fresh fact is to be added — here the carrying off of the head — and he points to the twice-told arrival of Joab (2 Sam. 3:22) and the repeated gathering at Hebron (2 Sam. 5:1) as parallels. Keil and Delitzsch agree the thread, broken by the explanatory aside of the previous verse, is resumed with the same words in order to attach the sequel: the murderers beheaded Ishbosheth, took the head, and went 'by the way of the Arabah,' the valley of the Jordan (2 Sam. 2:29), the whole night through, to bring it to David at Hebron.

The reformed Gill weighs a different reading of the sequence. Repeating the verse to gain a fuller account of the murder, he reports Abarbinel's view that the brothers entered twice: on the first entry they smote the king under the fifth rib and escaped, then, fearing they had not left him dead, returned to behead him. Whether one entry or two, the aim of the retelling is the same — to bring the severed head into the story.

The witnesses do not contend; together they trace the crime to its ghastly close and its long night-march. The detail of the head borne all night down the Jordan valley is not idle: it fixes the killers' purpose, for they carry their trophy the whole weary length of the Arabah to lay it at the feet of the man they expect to reward them. Keil and Delitzsch record the very words they rehearsed for the presentation — 'Behold the head of Ishbosheth, the son of Saul thine enemy, who sought thy life' — so that the long march was also a long preparation of the flattery they meant to speak. The historian lets the distance measure their calculation, and the reader is carried, with the head, straight to the throne-room scene of v.8, where their expectation will be shattered.
8:
The assassins present their trophy with a pious speech, and the witnesses expose the calculation beneath the words. The grammatical-historical commentators weigh the murderers' claim that 'the LORD hath avenged my lord the king.' Ellicott is careful: they did not pretend a divine commission for the deed, but 'meant to say that, in the providence of God, David was thus avenged on the seed of his cruel persecutor' — and they framed it so as to awaken his gratitude toward themselves. Keil and Delitzsch set the scene of the presentation, the head laid before David at Hebron with the flattering words, and note that David's reply will run exactly opposite to what the pair expect, recalling how he had already dealt at Ziklag with the Amalekite who thought Saul's death good news (2 Sam. 1:14).

The reformed Gill and the wesleyan-arminian Clarke read the men's psychology alike. Gill says they brought the head 'supposing it would have been a very acceptable present' to David; Clarke sees them, 'as did the poor lying Amalekite,' thinking to ingratiate themselves by an abominable act — the same fatal misreading of David's heart.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts the moment into a maxim of conscience. A man, he grants, may be glad to obtain his just wishes and yet sincerely regret the means by which they come; he may profit by another's death and still detest it. So David, though a gainer by Ishbosheth's fall, will not be beholden to murderers, for 'God had helped him over many a difficulty, and through many a danger, therefore he depended upon him to crown and complete his own work.' Henry hears in David's coming oath the settled confidence of a man who speaks of his redemption from all adversity as a thing already done, though many a storm still lay before him. Ellicott adds that the men state the fact of the murder 'in the way they thought best calculated to awaken the gratitude of David towards themselves,' dressing a private butchery in the language of divine vengeance. The witnesses agree: the killers have brought their own condemnation, and the very speech meant to earn a reward in v.9 seals their doom, for they have mistaken both the man and his God.
9:
David's answer opens the sentence of judgement, and the witnesses read it as justice, oath, and self-committal to God. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gives the fullest reading. The murderers needed no witness against them, for they gloried in the fact; David, now the chief magistrate and by office the avenger of blood, shows them the heinousness of the crime, and that blood cried from his hand for blood. Henry hears David ratify the sentence with an oath — 'As the LORD liveth, who hath redeemed my soul out of all adversity' — both to forestall any intercession for the criminals and 'piously to intimate that his dependence was upon God' for the throne, so that he would not be beholden to any man to help him to it by unlawful practices. God had redeemed him from all adversity hitherto; therefore he trusted him to crown and complete his own work, speaking of that redemption as a thing already done, 'because he knew that he who had delivered would deliver.'

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke dwells on the same clause as the heart of the reply. David's life had often been sought by murderers, yet the living God 'had always redeemed that life from all adversity,' and it is this very providence that now summons him to punish men of blood rather than to reward them. To lean on assassins would be to distrust the God who had never failed him.

The reformed Gill marks only that David answered the sons of Rimmon 'in a manner they did not expect' — a short phrase that carries the whole reversal. The men had rehearsed a speech of triumph; they receive a verdict of death. Henry notes further that David was the more resolved to prosecute because, having for reasons of state spared Joab, he would not now let a second murder go unrequited, and that the oath was framed to cut off any plea for mercy on the killers' behalf. The witnesses do not divide; they hear one voice in David's answer. He will not touch the crown with bloody hands, nor buy by murder what God has promised to give, and the precedent he cites in v.10 will show that this is no new principle but the settled rule of his reign.
10:
David grounds his verdict in a precedent, and the witnesses attend to what the case of the Amalekite does and does not prove. The grammatical-historical commentators read the citation closely. Ellicott corrects the English: the words 'thought that I would have given him' are not in the original, and the literal sense is rather 'which was the reward I gave him' — a bitter irony that lays bare David's view of the motive that had prompted the Amalekite's lie in 2 Sam. 1:10. Keil and Delitzsch set the precedent in its context: the man who announced Saul's death, expecting to be a messenger of good, David seized and slew at Ziklag; how much more will he requite murderers who kill a righteous man on his bed. Clarke adds a philological note dear to him — the Septuagint's word for the messenger's due is euangelia, so that the very term 'gospel' first signifies 'the reward which the bringer of good tidings is entitled to receive.'

The reformed Gill observes what the verse quietly concedes. David rehearses only that the Amalekite reported 'Saul is dead,' saying nothing of his having killed him; from which, with Abarbinel, Gill infers that the Amalekite had not in fact slain Saul, and that David executed him not for the killing but for the boast of it — for making himself, in hope of reward, the herald of a king's blood. Keil and Delitzsch, tracing David's construction, note that he calls the Amalekite one who 'thought he was a messenger of good tidings to me,' and yet was seized and slain — the more surely, then, will wicked men fare who have murdered a righteous man in his own house upon his bed.

The witnesses do not divide; the argument moves from the lesser to the greater. If a man who merely claimed to bring news of Saul's death met death for it at Ziklag, the two who have actually butchered a helpless king can look for nothing less. David's logic is a reproach as well as a sentence: he has never welcomed the bearer of a rival's blood, and he will not begin now. That he reasons from a case already closed shows the killers how badly they have read him; they took him for one who would prize a severed head, when he had long since made plain that he abhorred it. The full weight of the charge falls in v.11.
11:
The charge reaches its full weight in this verse, and the witnesses expound both the innocence of the victim and the pollution of the deed. The reformed Gill and the grammatical-historical Ellicott agree on the careful sense of 'a righteous person.' Gill grants that Ishbosheth was righteous in comparison with his murderers, though he had no just claim to the throne over against David or his own nephew Mephibosheth; his opposition to David, Gill judges, sprang not from malice but from ignorance, urged on by his friends. Ellicott makes the same limitation — Ishbosheth was 'righteous, not at fault, so far as the matter in hand and his relation to the assassins is concerned' — so that whatever his politics, he had done these men no wrong to warrant their knife.

Ellicott then draws out the verse's darker vocabulary. To 'take you away from the earth' means, more exactly, to put the murderers away out of the land; the word is one used of purging evil and its guilt from the land (Deut. 19:13), for the guilt of murder defiled the land until it was expiated by the execution of the murderer (Num. 35:33). Justice here is not merely punitive but cleansing.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke gathers the aggravations that make the crime so foul. Ishbosheth was innocent, and so gave none any ground of quarrel; he was in his own house, which should have been his sanctuary; he was upon his bed, resting in the heat of the day, unguarded and unsuspecting; and the men who slew him professed to hold him as their sovereign. 'To take away the life of such a man, in such circumstances,' Clarke concludes, was 'the most abandoned treachery.'

The witnesses do not contend; together they frame David's question as unanswerable. A blameless man, in his own home, on his own bed, killed by his own officers — every circumstance heightens the horror, and the land itself cries for cleansing. The requiring of blood at their hand is therefore no vengeance of David's but the demand of God's justice, executed in v.12.
12:
The chapter closes with execution and burial, and the witnesses read the doubled action as David's public disavowal of the whole bloody business. The grammatical-historical Ellicott sets the two halves side by side. The mutilation of the criminals — hands and feet cut off, the bodies hung up beside the pool of Hebron where all the people resorted — was a disgrace made as public as possible, 'a terrible warning against the commission of such crimes by others.' Keil and Delitzsch add the fitness of the mutilation: they severed 'the hands with which they had committed the murder, and the feet which had run for the reward.' Over against this stands the honour paid the victim: the head of Ishbosheth was buried, Ellicott notes, in the sepulchre of Abner, 'his chief friend and supporter,' so that the same day that shamed the murderers dignified the murdered.

The wesleyan-arminian Clarke reads the sentence as sincerity proved. None ever more richly deserved death, and 'by this act of justice, David showed to all Israel that he was a decided enemy to the destruction of Saul's family,' and that none could lift a hand against them without meeting condign punishment. In all this, Clarke insists, David could not have shown a stricter regard for justice.

The reformed Gill states the plain fact — David commanded his young men and they slew them — while the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry presses the warning home. The hands and feet were hung up in terror, that the deed might recommend David to the people as a ruler who cared not what blood he waded through to a crown. What a confusion, Henry marvels, was this to the two murderers, and what a disappointment! And he turns it upon every age: those who think to serve the Son of David by war, persecution, fraud, or rapine, crying 'Let the Lord be glorified' while they kill, will learn one day that Christianity was never meant to destroy humanity. The witnesses do not divide; they close the chapter on a king who would not stain his throne, and who leaves the reader, at 2 Sam. 5:1, ready to receive the crown from God's hand rather than from murderers'.
